SE1.tagline: Build and operate scalable cloud-native systems with confidence
SE1.subtitle: foundations of distributed systems, declarative orchestration, and the economics of elastic computation
foundations: CAP theorem and the availability, consistency, partition-tolerance trade-off
Quorum and eventual-consistency models for replicated state
Reconciliation and the declarative desired-state control loop
Process isolation through namespaces and cgroups
Queueing theory and tail-latency analysis under load
Autoscaling control theory and capacity versus cost models
Supply-chain provenance and reproducible-build theory

literature.title: A View of Cloud Computing
literature.authors: Michael Armbrust, Armando Fox, Rean Griffith, Anthony D. Joseph, Randy Katz, Andy Konwinski, Gunho Lee, David Patterson, Ariel Rabkin, Ion Stoica, Matei Zaharia

literature.title: CAP Twelve Years Later: How the Rules Have Changed
literature.authors: Eric Brewer

literature.title: Borg, Omega, and Kubernetes
literature.authors: Brendan Burns, Brian Grant, David Oppenheimer, Eric Brewer, John Wilkes

literature.title: Large-scale Cluster Management at Google with Borg
literature.authors: Abhishek Verma, Luis Pedrosa, Madhukar Korupolu, David Oppenheimer, Eric Tune, John Wilkes

literature.title: Dynamo: Amazon's Highly Available Key-value Store
literature.authors: Giuseppe DeCandia, Deniz Hastorun, Madan Jampani, Gunavardhan Kakulapati, Avinash Lakshman, Alex Pilchin, Swaminathan Sivasubramanian, Peter Vosshall, Werner Vogels

literature.title: The Google File System
literature.authors: Sanjay Ghemawat, Howard Gobioff, Shun-Tak Leung

SE1.build: Engineered a cloud-native multi-service platform deployed to Kubernetes with Helm and Istio, provisioned end to end as Terraform infrastructure code, autoscaled under k6 load tests, and instrumented with Prometheus and Grafana to a defined cost budget.
objectives: Explain cloud service models, regions, and the shared responsibility model
Analyze distributed-systems foundations of availability, consistency, and partition tolerance
Containerize applications and design minimal, reproducible images
Operate workloads on Kubernetes using Deployments, Services, and Ingress
Decompose systems into microservices and reason about coupling and resilience
Provision infrastructure declaratively using infrastructure as code
Configure autoscaling and load balancing to meet latency and availability targets
Evaluate cloud cost and the economic trade-offs of elasticity and reservation
Instrument clusters and services for metrics, logs, and traces
Justify architectural choices against scalability and reliability requirements
prerequisites: Basic Linux command line and networking
Familiarity with HTTP and web services

weeks.title: Cloud Computing Foundations
weeks.lecture: Survey cloud service models, virtualization, regions, and the CAP theorem framing of distributed trade-offs.
weeks.practice: Set up cloud accounts and tooling, then provision a first managed service and inspect its topology.
weeks.project: Project repository, cloud account, and a deployed hello-service baseline are established.

weeks.title: Containers and Docker
weeks.lecture: Examine Linux namespaces and cgroups, image layering, and the theory behind reproducible builds.
weeks.practice: Write Dockerfiles for the project services and run them locally with volumes and networks.
weeks.project: Core service is containerized with a clean, layered, reproducible image.

weeks.title: Registries and Image Hygiene
weeks.lecture: Discuss supply-chain provenance, image scanning, and the economics of small immutable artifacts.
weeks.practice: Tag, scan, and publish project images to a registry and pin reproducible base images.
weeks.project: Versioned, scanned images are published to a registry for the project.

weeks.title: Kubernetes Architecture
weeks.lecture: Study the control plane, reconciliation loops, and the declarative desired-state model.
weeks.practice: Deploy the project to a cluster using Pods, Deployments, and Services.
weeks.project: Project runs on Kubernetes with declarative manifests under version control.

weeks.title: Specification Milestone
weeks.lecture: Review configuration, secrets, and persistent storage abstractions for stateful workloads.
weeks.practice: Student teams present their project specification: scope, target architecture, scaling goals, and cost budget.
weeks.project: Approved specification document and cluster configuration plan are delivered.

weeks.title: Networking and Ingress
weeks.lecture: Analyze service discovery, the Kubernetes networking model, and ingress routing theory.
weeks.practice: Expose project services through Ingress controllers and apply network policies.
weeks.project: Project is reachable externally through managed ingress with policy controls.

weeks.title: Packaging with Helm
weeks.lecture: Discuss templating, configuration drift, and release management for declarative systems.
weeks.practice: Package the project as a versioned Helm chart with environment overrides.
weeks.project: Project ships as a parameterized, versioned Helm release.

weeks.title: Interim Demo Milestone
weeks.lecture: Cover microservice decomposition, bounded contexts, and inter-service resilience patterns.
weeks.practice: Student teams present an interim demo of the running multi-service deployment and discuss design trade-offs.
weeks.project: Working multi-service deployment is demonstrated with documented architecture.

weeks.title: Service Meshes
weeks.lecture: Examine sidecar proxies, mutual TLS, retries, and circuit breaking as reliability mechanisms.
weeks.practice: Add a service mesh to project traffic with observability and mTLS enabled.
weeks.project: Inter-service traffic is secured and observable through a mesh.

weeks.title: Infrastructure as Code
weeks.lecture: Discuss declarative provisioning, state management, and idempotency in infrastructure tooling.
weeks.practice: Reprovision the project environment from Terraform code with managed state.
weeks.project: Entire environment is reproducible from versioned infrastructure code.

weeks.title: Autoscaling and Load Balancing
weeks.lecture: Analyze horizontal and vertical scaling, queueing theory intuition, and load distribution.
weeks.practice: Configure autoscaling and load testing to meet defined latency targets.
weeks.project: Project scales automatically under load within latency objectives.

weeks.title: Observability and Cost
weeks.lecture: Cover metrics, tracing, SLOs, and the cost models of elastic versus reserved capacity.
weeks.practice: Add dashboards and alerts, then produce a cost analysis with optimization actions.
weeks.project: Observability stack and a cost optimization report are in place.

weeks.title: Final Demo and Defense
weeks.lecture: Synthesize cloud-native architecture principles and review the path from container to scaled service.
weeks.practice: Student teams present the final demo with an oral defense of architecture, scaling, and cost decisions.
weeks.project: Final scalable cloud-native system is delivered with documentation and defense.

project.summary: Teams build one cloud-native application and carry it from a single container to a scaled, observable, multi-service deployment on Kubernetes. The system is provisioned entirely as infrastructure code, secured and observed through a service mesh, and tuned for autoscaling within a defined cost budget.

tools: Docker: build and run application containers
Kubernetes: orchestrate containerized workloads
Helm: package and template Kubernetes manifests
Istio: manage service to service traffic and policy
Terraform: provision cloud infrastructure as code
Prometheus: collect cluster and application metrics
Grafana: visualize metrics and build dashboards
Amazon Web Services: host managed cloud services
kubectl: operate clusters from the command line
Trivy: scan container images for vulnerabilities
k6: run load and performance tests

references.title: Designing Distributed Systems, 2nd Edition: Patterns and Paradigms for Scalable, Reliable Services
Kubernetes: Up and Running, 3rd Edition
references.authors: Brendan Burns, Joe Beda, Kelsey Hightower, Lachlan Evenson

references.title: Building Microservices, 2nd Edition: Designing Fine-Grained Systems
Terraform: Up and Running, 3rd Edition: Writing Infrastructure as Code
Cloud Native Patterns: Designing Change-Tolerant Software

SE2.tagline: Engineer software that is secure, tested, and reliable by design
SE2.subtitle: principles of secure-by-design engineering, fault-based testing theory, and reliability under formal objectives
foundations: Saltzer and Schroeder protection principles and least privilege
STRIDE threat modeling over data-flow trust boundaries
Coverage criteria and fault-based testing theory
Static data-flow and taint analysis with soundness trade-offs
Trusting-trust and supply-chain integrity reasoning
Service level objectives, error budgets, and reliability theory
High-cardinality telemetry and the three pillars of observability

literature.title: The Protection of Information in Computer Systems
literature.authors: Jerome H. Saltzer, Michael D. Schroeder

literature.title: Reflections on Trusting Trust
literature.authors: Ken Thompson

literature.title: Hints on Test Data Selection: Help for the Practicing Programmer
literature.authors: Richard A. DeMillo, Richard J. Lipton, Frederick G. Sayward

literature.title: Why Cryptosystems Fail
literature.authors: Ross Anderson

literature.title: OWASP Top 10:2021
literature.authors: OWASP Foundation

literature.title: Secure Software Development Framework (SSDF) Version 1.1, NIST SP 800-218
literature.authors: Murugiah Souppaya, Karen Scarfone, Donna Dodson

SE2.build: Hardened a web application end to end: threat-modeled it with OWASP Threat Dragon, remediated OWASP Top 10 risks, and built a GitHub Actions CI pipeline gating on Semgrep SAST, OWASP ZAP DAST, and dependency scanning, with OpenTelemetry, Prometheus, and Grafana enforcing service level objectives.
objectives: Explain the principles of secure software development across the lifecycle
Analyze the OWASP Top 10 risks and apply secure coding countermeasures
Construct threat models using data-flow diagrams and trust boundaries
Evaluate dependency and supply-chain risks and apply provenance controls
Apply SAST and DAST tooling and interpret their findings critically
Design automated test suites grounded in coverage and fault-based theory
Build continuous integration and delivery pipelines with quality gates
Conduct effective code reviews focused on correctness and security
Instrument systems for observability with metrics, logs, and traces
Reason about reliability using service level objectives and error budgets

weeks.title: Secure Development and Threats
weeks.lecture: Introduce security principles, the CIA triad, attacker models, and the secure development lifecycle.
weeks.practice: Audit the starter project, enumerate assets, and set up the repository and toolchain.
weeks.project: Project repository, threat scope, and security baseline are established.

weeks.title: OWASP Top 10 and Secure Coding
weeks.lecture: Examine injection, broken access control, and cryptographic failures with their root causes.
weeks.practice: Find and fix seeded OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities in the project code.
weeks.project: Project passes a first pass of OWASP Top 10 remediation.

weeks.title: Threat Modeling
weeks.lecture: Teach STRIDE, data-flow diagrams, trust boundaries, and risk prioritization theory.
weeks.practice: Produce a threat model of the project with prioritized mitigations.
weeks.project: A documented threat model with a mitigation backlog is delivered.

weeks.title: Software Testing Foundations
weeks.lecture: Cover test oracles, coverage criteria, equivalence partitioning, and fault-based testing theory.
weeks.practice: Write unit and integration tests guided by coverage criteria for the project.
weeks.project: Project gains a baseline automated test suite with measured coverage.

weeks.title: Specification Milestone
weeks.lecture: Review test strategy, security requirements, and how quality attributes shape design.
weeks.practice: Student teams present their project specification: security requirements, threat model, and test and reliability plan.
weeks.project: Approved specification with security and quality goals is delivered.

weeks.title: Continuous Integration and Delivery
weeks.lecture: Discuss pipeline theory, deployment automation, and the economics of fast feedback.
weeks.practice: Build a CI pipeline that runs tests and blocks merges on failures.
weeks.project: Project has a CI pipeline enforcing tests on every change.

weeks.title: SAST and Static Analysis
weeks.lecture: Explain static analysis theory, data-flow and taint tracking, and false-positive trade-offs.
weeks.practice: Integrate a SAST scanner into the pipeline and triage its findings.
weeks.project: Pipeline runs static security analysis as a gated stage.

weeks.title: Interim Demo Milestone
weeks.lecture: Cover dynamic analysis and fuzzing concepts and how they complement static testing.
weeks.practice: Student teams present an interim demo of the secured project, its pipeline, and current findings.
weeks.project: Secured project with working CI and analysis is demonstrated.

weeks.title: DAST and Dependency Security
weeks.lecture: Examine dynamic scanning, software composition analysis, and supply-chain provenance models.
weeks.practice: Run DAST against the project and add dependency and SBOM scanning.
weeks.project: Project enforces dynamic and dependency security checks.

weeks.title: Code Review and Secure Authentication
weeks.lecture: Discuss review effectiveness research, authentication, session management, and access control design.
weeks.practice: Conduct structured peer code reviews and harden authentication in the project.
weeks.project: Project has reviewed code and hardened authentication and access control.

weeks.title: Reliability and SRE
weeks.lecture: Introduce service level objectives, error budgets, and reliability engineering principles.
weeks.practice: Define SLOs for the project and add resilience to a critical path.
weeks.project: Project defines SLOs and improves a critical-path failure mode.

weeks.title: Observability
weeks.lecture: Cover the three pillars of observability and the theory of high-cardinality telemetry.
weeks.practice: Instrument the project with metrics, logs, and traces plus alerting.
weeks.project: Project emits structured telemetry with dashboards and alerts.

weeks.title: Final Demo and Defense
weeks.lecture: Synthesize secure, tested, and reliable engineering and review the full quality pipeline.
weeks.practice: Student teams present the final demo with an oral defense of security, testing, and reliability decisions.
weeks.project: Final secure and reliable system is delivered with documentation and defense.

project.summary: Teams take one web application and engineer it to be secure, tested, and reliable end to end. They threat-model it, remediate OWASP Top 10 risks, build a CI and CD pipeline with SAST, DAST, and dependency scanning, and add observability and service level objectives. Application security is revisited every week as new attack surfaces appear.

tools: JUnit or pytest: write unit and integration tests
GitHub Actions: run continuous integration pipelines
OWASP ZAP: perform dynamic application security testing
Semgrep: run static application security testing
Snyk: scan dependencies for known vulnerabilities
OWASP Dependency-Check: detect vulnerable libraries
Trivy: scan images and generate software bill of materials
OWASP Threat Dragon: build and document threat models
OpenTelemetry: instrument services for traces and metrics
Prometheus: collect reliability metrics and define alerts
Grafana: visualize service level objectives and telemetry

references.title: OWASP Top 10 Web Application Security Risks
Web Application Security: Exploitation and Countermeasures for Modern Web Applications, 2nd Edition
Continuous Delivery: Reliable Software Releases through Build, Test, and Deployment Automation
Site Reliability Engineering: How Google Runs Production Systems
references.authors: Betsy Beyer, Chris Jones, Jennifer Petoff, Niall Richard Murphy

references.title: Observability Engineering: Achieving Production Excellence
references.authors: Charity Majors, Liz Fong-Jones, George Miranda

references.title: Secure Software Development Framework (SSDF) Version 1.1, NIST SP 800-218
references.authors: National Institute of Standards and Technology

SE3.tagline: Craft fast, accessible, component-driven web interfaces
SE3.subtitle: the component model, declarative rendering, reactive state, and the theory of accessible performant interfaces
foundations: The document object model and the browser rendering pipeline
The component model and unidirectional data flow
Declarative rendering, reconciliation, and the virtual DOM
Reactive programming and dependency-tracked state propagation
WCAG accessibility principles and semantic, inclusive interaction
The critical rendering path and front-end performance metrics
Module bundling, tree shaking, and code splitting

literature.title: Architectural Styles and the Design of Network-based Software Architectures
literature.authors: Roy Thomas Fielding

literature.title: A Survey on Reactive Programming
literature.authors: Engineer Bainomugisha, Andoni Lombide Carreton, Tom Van Cutsem, Stijn Mostinckx, Wolfgang De Meuter

literature.title: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.2, W3C Recommendation
literature.authors: W3C Accessibility Guidelines Working Group

literature.title: HTML Standard (Living Standard)
literature.authors: WHATWG

literature.title: DOM Standard (Living Standard)
literature.authors: WHATWG

literature.title: ECMAScript Language Specification (ECMA-262)
literature.authors: Ecma International, TC39

SE3.build: Shipped an accessible single-page application in React or Vue with TypeScript: a composed component hierarchy, predictable store-managed state, client-side routing, WCAG-audited markup, and a Vite code-split build tuned to performance budgets and covered by Vitest and Playwright tests.
objectives: Explain the component model and unidirectional data flow in modern frameworks
Analyze the document object model, rendering, and reconciliation foundations
Build component-based interfaces with React or Vue
Manage application state with predictable patterns and stores
Design responsive layouts using modern CSS and design principles
Apply accessibility standards grounded in the WCAG guidelines
Configure modern build tooling and module bundling
Measure and optimize front-end performance and loading behavior
Test components and user interactions systematically
Evaluate trade-offs between rendering strategies and architectures

weeks.title: The Web Platform and DOM
weeks.lecture: Examine the browser rendering pipeline, the DOM, and the event model as core abstractions.
weeks.practice: Set up the toolchain and build a small DOM-driven page for the project shell.
weeks.project: Project scaffold and initial static interface are established.

weeks.title: Modern JavaScript
weeks.lecture: Cover modules, asynchronous programming, and the language features behind reactive UIs.
weeks.practice: Refactor the project shell into modular JavaScript with async data loading.
weeks.project: Project loads data asynchronously into a modular front end.

weeks.title: Component Model and React or Vue
weeks.lecture: Introduce the component model, declarative rendering, and unidirectional data flow theory.
weeks.practice: Rebuild the interface as components in React or Vue.
weeks.project: Project interface is implemented as reusable components.

weeks.title: Props, Events, and Composition
weeks.lecture: Discuss component composition, data binding, and the design of clean component APIs.
weeks.practice: Compose the project from nested components with clear prop and event contracts.
weeks.project: Project features a composed component hierarchy with clear interfaces.

weeks.title: Specification Milestone
weeks.lecture: Review state management theory and how component boundaries shape architecture.
weeks.practice: Student teams present their project specification: feature scope, component map, and accessibility and performance goals.
weeks.project: Approved specification and component architecture plan are delivered.

weeks.title: State Management
weeks.lecture: Analyze local versus global state, stores, and predictable state-update patterns.
weeks.practice: Introduce a state store and centralize shared project state.
weeks.project: Project manages shared state through a predictable store.

weeks.title: Routing and Data Fetching
weeks.lecture: Cover client-side routing, data caching, and synchronization with server state.
weeks.practice: Add routing and resilient data fetching with loading and error states.
weeks.project: Project supports multiple routes with robust data fetching.

weeks.title: Interim Demo Milestone
weeks.lecture: Discuss forms, validation, and managing complex interactive state.
weeks.practice: Student teams present an interim demo of the working component application and discuss design choices.
weeks.project: Interactive multi-view application is demonstrated.

weeks.title: Responsive Design
weeks.lecture: Examine responsive layout theory, fluid grids, and mobile-first design principles.
weeks.practice: Make the project fully responsive across breakpoints with modern CSS.
weeks.project: Project adapts cleanly across mobile and desktop layouts.

weeks.title: Accessibility
weeks.lecture: Teach WCAG principles, semantic markup, ARIA, and inclusive interaction design.
weeks.practice: Audit and remediate the project against accessibility standards.
weeks.project: Project meets core accessibility guidelines with verified fixes.

weeks.title: Build Tooling and Bundling
weeks.lecture: Explain module bundling, tree shaking, code splitting, and dev-server theory.
weeks.practice: Optimize the project build with Vite, code splitting, and asset handling.
weeks.project: Project has an optimized, code-split production build.

weeks.title: Performance and Testing
weeks.lecture: Cover web performance metrics, the critical rendering path, and component testing strategy.
weeks.practice: Profile and tune the project performance and add component and interaction tests.
weeks.project: Project meets performance budgets and has an automated test suite.

weeks.title: Final Demo and Defense
weeks.lecture: Synthesize front-end architecture, accessibility, and performance principles.
weeks.practice: Student teams present the final demo with an oral defense of component design, accessibility, and performance decisions.
weeks.project: Final front-end application is delivered with documentation and defense.

project.summary: Teams design and build one single-page web application that grows from a static shell into a component-based, state-managed product. The application is made responsive, accessible to WCAG standards, and tuned to performance budgets with an optimized build. Every increment is validated through component and interaction tests.

tools: React: build component-based user interfaces
Vue: build reactive component interfaces
TypeScript: add static typing to front-end code
Vite: bundle and serve the application during development
Redux Toolkit or Pinia: manage application state
React Router or Vue Router: handle client-side routing
Tailwind CSS: compose responsive utility-based styles
Vitest: run unit and component tests
Playwright: run end-to-end interaction tests
axe DevTools: audit accessibility compliance
Lighthouse: measure performance and best practices

SE4.tagline: Design robust back-end services, APIs, and data layers
SE4.subtitle: resource-oriented architecture, the relational model, transactional theory, and consistency under scale
foundations: REST architectural constraints and resource-oriented design
The relational model and normalization theory
ACID transactions, isolation levels, and indexing internals
Token and federated identity flows for authentication
Cache coherence, invalidation, and consistency trade-offs
Replication, statelessness, and the CAP trade-off
Role and attribute-based access control and least privilege

literature.title: Architectural Styles and the Design of Network-based Software Architectures
literature.authors: Roy Thomas Fielding

literature.title: A Relational Model of Data for Large Shared Data Banks
literature.authors: Edgar F. Codd

literature.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics
literature.authors: Roy Fielding, Mark Nottingham, Julian Reschke

literature.title: RFC 6749: The OAuth 2.0 Authorization Framework
literature.authors: Dick Hardt

literature.title: RFC 7519: JSON Web Token (JWT)
literature.authors: Michael Jones, John Bradley, Nat Sakimura

literature.title: Dynamo: Amazon's Highly Available Key-value Store
literature.authors: Giuseppe DeCandia, Deniz Hastorun, Madan Jampani, Gunavardhan Kakulapati, Avinash Lakshman, Alex Pilchin, Swaminathan Sivasubramanian, Peter Vosshall, Werner Vogels

SE4.build: Built a back-end API platform on Node.js or FastAPI exposing both REST and an Apollo GraphQL layer over a normalized, indexed PostgreSQL schema, secured with JWT authentication and role-based access control, accelerated with Redis caching, and load-tested with k6 against scaling targets.
objectives: Explain the architectural constraints of REST and resource-oriented design
Compare REST and GraphQL and reason about their trade-offs
Model relational and non-relational data and analyze normalization
Design and implement APIs with versioning and clear contracts
Implement authentication, sessions, and authorization securely
Apply caching strategies and reason about consistency and invalidation
Analyze database indexing, transactions, and query performance
Scale back-end services horizontally and handle concurrency
Validate APIs with automated and contract testing
Evaluate consistency, availability, and latency trade-offs in data systems
prerequisites: Familiarity with HTTP and web fundamentals

weeks.title: Web Services and HTTP
weeks.lecture: Examine HTTP semantics, statelessness, and the architectural style behind web services.
weeks.practice: Scaffold the project service and implement a first set of HTTP endpoints.
weeks.project: Project service skeleton with basic endpoints is established.

weeks.title: REST and Resource Design
weeks.lecture: Cover REST constraints, resource modeling, hypermedia, and the Richardson maturity model.
weeks.practice: Design RESTful resources and routes for the project domain.
weeks.project: Project exposes a coherent RESTful resource model.

weeks.title: Data Modeling and Relational Databases
weeks.lecture: Teach the relational model, normalization theory, and entity-relationship design.
weeks.practice: Design the project schema and connect the service to a relational database.
weeks.project: Project persists data through a normalized relational schema.

weeks.title: Queries, Indexing, and Transactions
weeks.lecture: Analyze query execution, indexing, and ACID transactions with isolation levels.
weeks.practice: Add indexes, transactions, and tuned queries to the project data layer.
weeks.project: Project data access is transactional and performance-tuned.

weeks.title: Specification Milestone
weeks.lecture: Review API contracts and how data modeling shapes service architecture.
weeks.practice: Student teams present their project specification: domain model, API contract, and scaling goals.
weeks.project: Approved specification with data model and API contract is delivered.

weeks.title: GraphQL
weeks.lecture: Examine the GraphQL type system, resolvers, and trade-offs against REST.
weeks.practice: Add a GraphQL layer over the project domain with schema and resolvers.
weeks.project: Project offers a GraphQL interface alongside its REST API.

weeks.title: API Design, Versioning, and Validation
weeks.lecture: Discuss contract design, versioning strategies, pagination, and error modeling.
weeks.practice: Add validation, pagination, and versioning to the project APIs.
weeks.project: Project APIs are validated, paginated, and versioned.

weeks.title: Interim Demo Milestone
weeks.lecture: Cover authentication models, tokens, and session management foundations.
weeks.practice: Student teams present an interim demo of the project APIs and data layer.
weeks.project: Working API and data layer are demonstrated.

weeks.title: Authentication and Sessions
weeks.lecture: Examine session versus token authentication, OAuth, and OpenID Connect flows.
weeks.practice: Implement secure authentication and session handling in the project.
weeks.project: Project enforces authenticated, session-aware access.

weeks.title: Authorization and Access Control
weeks.lecture: Discuss role and attribute-based access control and least-privilege design.
weeks.practice: Add fine-grained authorization rules to project endpoints.
weeks.project: Project enforces role-based access control across resources.

weeks.title: Caching
weeks.lecture: Analyze caching layers, invalidation strategies, and consistency trade-offs.
weeks.practice: Add response and data caching with an invalidation strategy to the project.
weeks.project: Project serves cached responses with controlled invalidation.

weeks.title: Scaling and Concurrency
weeks.lecture: Cover horizontal scaling, statelessness, replication, and the CAP trade-off.
weeks.practice: Load test the project and add scaling and concurrency safeguards.
weeks.project: Project sustains load through scaling and concurrency control.

weeks.title: Final Demo and Defense
weeks.lecture: Synthesize API, data, security, and scaling principles for back-end systems.
weeks.practice: Student teams present the final demo with an oral defense of API, data, and scaling decisions.
weeks.project: Final back-end system is delivered with documentation and defense.

project.summary: Teams build one back-end service that grows from basic endpoints into a complete API platform with a tuned data layer. The service exposes both REST and GraphQL, enforces secure authentication and authorization, and adds caching and scaling to meet load targets. Each increment is validated with automated and contract tests.

tools: Node.js or Python: implement back-end services
Express or FastAPI: build HTTP API frameworks
PostgreSQL: store relational data
Prisma or SQLAlchemy: model and query the database
Apollo Server: serve a GraphQL API
Redis: cache responses and session data
JSON Web Tokens: implement token-based authentication
OpenAPI: document and validate REST contracts
Postman: explore and test APIs
Docker: run databases and services locally
k6: load test back-end endpoints

references.title: RESTful Web APIs: Services for a Changing World
references.authors: Leonard Richardson, Mike Amundsen, Sam Ruby

references.title: Learning GraphQL: Declarative Data Fetching for Modern Web Apps
Database System Concepts, 7th Edition
references.authors: Abraham Silberschatz, Henry F. Korth, S. Sudarshan

references.title: OpenID Connect Core 1.0
references.authors: N. Sakimura, J. Bradley, M. Jones, B. de Medeiros, C. Mortimore

SE6.tagline: Build software for mobile, IoT, and the edge
SE6.subtitle: computation across device, edge, and cloud under constraints, intermittency, and eventual consistency
foundations: Ubiquitous and pervasive computing models
Publish-subscribe messaging and quality-of-service semantics
Constrained-device RESTful communication and protocol trade-offs
Edge and fog computing latency and bandwidth economics
Local-first design and eventual consistency
Conflict-free replicated data types and merge theory
Synchronization protocols and conflict detection

literature.title: The Computer for the 21st Century
literature.authors: Mark Weiser

literature.title: The Case for VM-Based Cloudlets in Mobile Computing
literature.authors: Mahadev Satyanarayanan, Paramvir Bahl, Ramon Caceres, Nigel Davies

literature.title: Fog Computing and Its Role in the Internet of Things
literature.authors: Flavio Bonomi, Rodolfo Milito, Jiang Zhu, Sateesh Addepalli

literature.title: Conflict-free Replicated Data Types
literature.authors: Marc Shapiro, Nuno Preguica, Carlos Baquero, Marek Zawirski

literature.title: Local-first Software: You Own Your Data, in Spite of the Cloud
literature.authors: Martin Kleppmann, Adam Wiggins, Peter van Hardenberg, Mark McGranaghan

literature.title: RFC 7252: The Constrained Application Protocol (CoAP)
literature.authors: Zach Shelby, Klaus Hartke, Carsten Bormann

SE6.build: Delivered a device-to-cloud system pairing a Flutter cross-platform app with IoT sensors over MQTT and CoAP, a Raspberry Pi edge node that filters and aggregates readings, and offline-first local storage that synchronizes with conflict resolution over secured channels.
objectives: Explain the architecture of mobile, IoT, and edge computing systems
Build cross-platform mobile applications with a modern framework
Analyze mobile UI, lifecycle, and resource-constrained design
Apply IoT messaging protocols such as MQTT and CoAP
Integrate sensors and actuators with constrained devices
Design edge computing architectures and reason about latency trade-offs
Implement offline-first applications with local storage
Apply data synchronization and conflict resolution strategies
Evaluate security and privacy concerns across device boundaries
Assess trade-offs between cloud, edge, and device computation

weeks.title: Mobile and Edge Landscape
weeks.lecture: Survey mobile, IoT, and edge architectures and the constraints of distributed devices.
weeks.practice: Set up the toolchain and build a first cross-platform mobile app skeleton.
weeks.project: Project mobile app skeleton runs on a device or emulator.

weeks.title: Cross-Platform UI and Lifecycle
weeks.lecture: Examine widget composition, the app lifecycle, and resource-aware UI design.
weeks.practice: Build the core project screens and navigation with a cross-platform framework.
weeks.project: Project app has core navigable screens.

weeks.title: State, Storage, and Device APIs
weeks.lecture: Discuss local persistence, permissions, and access to device sensors and capabilities.
weeks.practice: Add local storage and device sensor access to the project app.
weeks.project: Project app stores data locally and reads a device sensor.

weeks.title: IoT Architecture and Sensors
weeks.lecture: Cover IoT system layers, constrained devices, and sensing and actuation foundations.
weeks.practice: Connect a simulated or physical sensor and publish readings from the project.
weeks.project: Project ingests readings from an IoT sensor source.

weeks.title: Specification Milestone
weeks.lecture: Review IoT messaging protocols and how device constraints shape architecture.
weeks.practice: Student teams present their project specification: device topology, protocols, and offline and sync goals.
weeks.project: Approved specification with device and protocol plan is delivered.

weeks.title: MQTT and Publish-Subscribe
weeks.lecture: Examine the publish-subscribe model, MQTT topics, and quality-of-service levels.
weeks.practice: Wire the project devices and app through an MQTT broker.
weeks.project: Project communicates over MQTT publish-subscribe messaging.

weeks.title: CoAP and Constrained Protocols
weeks.lecture: Discuss CoAP, RESTful constrained communication, and protocol trade-offs for low-power devices.
weeks.practice: Add a CoAP interaction and compare it with MQTT in the project.
weeks.project: Project supports a constrained-protocol communication path.

weeks.title: Interim Demo Milestone
weeks.lecture: Introduce edge computing models and the division of work across device, edge, and cloud.
weeks.practice: Student teams present an interim demo of the mobile and IoT system end to end.
weeks.project: Connected mobile and IoT system is demonstrated.

weeks.title: Edge Computing
weeks.lecture: Analyze edge architectures, latency, bandwidth, and the economics of local computation.
weeks.practice: Add an edge processing component that filters or aggregates device data.
weeks.project: Project processes data at the edge before reaching the cloud.

weeks.title: Offline-First Design
weeks.lecture: Examine local-first principles, offline state, and the theory of eventual consistency.
weeks.practice: Make the project app fully functional offline with a local data store.
weeks.project: Project app works offline and queues changes locally.

weeks.title: Data Synchronization
weeks.lecture: Cover synchronization protocols, conflict detection, and reconciliation strategies.
weeks.practice: Implement sync between offline clients and the backend in the project.
weeks.project: Project synchronizes local and remote data reliably.

weeks.title: Conflict Resolution and Security
weeks.lecture: Discuss conflict-free replicated data types, merge strategies, and device security and privacy.
weeks.practice: Add conflict resolution and secure the project communication channels.
weeks.project: Project resolves sync conflicts over secured channels.

weeks.title: Final Demo and Defense
weeks.lecture: Synthesize mobile, IoT, edge, and synchronization principles into a coherent design.
weeks.practice: Student teams present the final demo with an oral defense of architecture, protocol, and sync decisions.
weeks.project: Final mobile and IoT edge system is delivered with documentation and defense.

project.summary: Teams build one connected system spanning a cross-platform mobile app, IoT sensors, and an edge processing component. The system communicates over MQTT and CoAP, processes data at the edge, and works offline with reliable synchronization and conflict resolution. Each increment extends the device-to-cloud pipeline.

tools: Flutter: build cross-platform mobile apps
Dart: program Flutter applications
React Native: build native mobile apps from JavaScript
SQLite: store data locally on devices
Eclipse Mosquitto: run an MQTT broker
MQTT.js or paho-mqtt: publish and subscribe over MQTT
libcoap or aiocoap: communicate over CoAP
Node-RED: prototype IoT and edge data flows
Raspberry Pi: host an edge computing node
PowerSync or CouchDB: synchronize offline data
Firebase: provide backend messaging and storage

references.title: RFC 7252: The Constrained Application Protocol (CoAP)
references.authors: Z. Shelby, K. Hartke, C. Bormann

references.title: Edge Computing: Vision and Challenges
references.authors: Weisong Shi, Jie Cao, Quan Zhang, Youhuizi Li, Lanyu Xu

references.title: Local-first software: You own your data, in spite of the cloud
references.authors: Martin Kleppmann, Adam Wiggins, Peter van Hardenberg, Mark McGranaghan

SE7.tagline: Architect data-intensive and AI-driven systems at scale
SE7.subtitle: data systems theory, distributed consensus, streaming dataflow, and the operational debt of machine learning
foundations: Reliability, scalability, and maintainability as system properties
Storage-engine internals and data-model trade-offs
The MapReduce model of large-scale batch computation
Event-time dataflow, watermarks, and windowing theory
Replication, partitioning, and the CAP and PACELC trade-offs
Distributed consensus and exactly-once processing semantics
Hidden technical debt and operational concerns in ML systems

literature.title: MapReduce: Simplified Data Processing on Large Clusters
literature.authors: Jeffrey Dean, Sanjay Ghemawat

literature.title: Dynamo: Amazon's Highly Available Key-value Store
literature.authors: Giuseppe DeCandia, Deniz Hastorun, Madan Jampani, Gunavardhan Kakulapati, Avinash Lakshman, Alex Pilchin, Swaminathan Sivasubramanian, Peter Vosshall, Werner Vogels

literature.title: Spanner: Google's Globally-Distributed Database
literature.authors: James C. Corbett, Jeffrey Dean, Michael Epstein, Andrew Fikes, Christopher Frost, J. J. Furman, Sanjay Ghemawat, and others

literature.title: In Search of an Understandable Consensus Algorithm (Raft)
literature.authors: Diego Ongaro, John Ousterhout

literature.title: The Dataflow Model: A Practical Approach to Balancing Correctness, Latency, and Cost in Massive-Scale, Unbounded, Out-of-Order Data Processing
literature.authors: Tyler Akidau, Robert Bradshaw, Craig Chambers, Slava Chernyak, Rafael J. Fernandez-Moctezuma, Reuven Lax, Sam McVeety, Daniel Mills, Frances Perry, Eric Schmidt, Sam Whittle

literature.title: Hidden Technical Debt in Machine Learning Systems
literature.authors: D. Sculley, Gary Holt, Daniel Golovin, Eugene Davydov, Todd Phillips, Dietmar Ebner, Vinay Chaudhary, Michael Young, Jean-Francois Crespo, Dan Dennison

SE7.build: Architected a data-intensive system that ingests events through Apache Kafka, processes them with Spark batch and Flink streaming pipelines into a replicated, partitioned Cassandra store, serves predictions from an MLflow-tracked model, and guards quality with Evidently drift monitoring.
objectives: Explain the foundations of reliability, scalability, and maintainability
Analyze data models, storage engines, and indexing trade-offs
Design batch and streaming data pipelines
Reason about distributed storage, replication, and partitioning
Apply the theory of consistency, consensus, and the CAP trade-off
Integrate machine learning models into production systems
Evaluate architectural trade-offs for AI-based and data-intensive workloads
Address technical debt and operational concerns in ML systems
Design for fault tolerance and exactly-once processing semantics
Assess data quality, lineage, and governance in pipelines

weeks.title: Data-Intensive System Foundations
weeks.lecture: Introduce reliability, scalability, and maintainability and the challenges of data at scale.
weeks.practice: Set up the project environment and define the data domain and pipeline goals.
weeks.project: Project repository and data-system design baseline are established.

weeks.title: Data Models and Storage Engines
weeks.lecture: Examine relational, document, and graph models and the internals of storage engines.
weeks.practice: Model the project data and select appropriate storage for its workload.
weeks.project: Project data model and storage choice are implemented and justified.

weeks.title: Encoding and Data Movement
weeks.lecture: Discuss serialization formats, schema evolution, and the dataflow between systems.
weeks.practice: Define schemas and ingest raw data into the project storage layer.
weeks.project: Project ingests and stores data with a versioned schema.

weeks.title: Batch Processing
weeks.lecture: Cover the batch processing model, MapReduce, and the theory of large-scale computation.
weeks.practice: Build a batch pipeline that transforms project data into derived datasets.
weeks.project: Project produces derived datasets through a batch pipeline.

weeks.title: Specification Milestone
weeks.lecture: Review streaming concepts and how architecture choices shape data systems.
weeks.practice: Student teams present their project specification: data sources, pipeline architecture, and scaling goals.
weeks.project: Approved specification with pipeline architecture is delivered.

weeks.title: Stream Processing
weeks.lecture: Examine streaming systems, event time versus processing time, and windowing theory.
weeks.practice: Add a streaming pipeline that processes project events in near real time.
weeks.project: Project processes events through a streaming pipeline.

weeks.title: Replication and Partitioning
weeks.lecture: Analyze replication strategies, partitioning, and rebalancing in distributed stores.
weeks.practice: Configure replication and partitioning for the project data store.
weeks.project: Project data store is replicated and partitioned for scale.

weeks.title: Interim Demo Milestone
weeks.lecture: Cover consistency models, consensus, and the CAP and PACELC trade-offs.
weeks.practice: Student teams present an interim demo of the data pipeline and storage layer.
weeks.project: Working pipeline and distributed storage are demonstrated.

weeks.title: Consistency and Fault Tolerance
weeks.lecture: Discuss transactions across nodes, exactly-once semantics, and fault-tolerance design.
weeks.practice: Add fault tolerance and delivery guarantees to the project pipeline.
weeks.project: Project pipeline tolerates faults with defined delivery guarantees.

weeks.title: ML System Integration
weeks.lecture: Examine the anatomy of ML systems, feature pipelines, and serving architectures.
weeks.practice: Integrate a trained model into the project as a serving component.
weeks.project: Project serves predictions from an integrated ML model.

weeks.title: Feature Stores and Data Quality
weeks.lecture: Discuss feature engineering pipelines, data lineage, and the cost of poor data quality.
weeks.practice: Add feature processing and data-quality checks to the project pipeline.
weeks.project: Project enforces data-quality checks feeding the model.

weeks.title: Technical Debt and Operations
weeks.lecture: Analyze hidden technical debt in ML systems, monitoring, and drift over time.
weeks.practice: Add monitoring and drift detection to the project ML pipeline.
weeks.project: Project monitors model and pipeline health in operation.

weeks.title: Final Demo and Defense
weeks.lecture: Synthesize data-intensive and AI system architecture and review the trade-offs made.
weeks.practice: Student teams present the final demo with an oral defense of architecture, scaling, and ML integration decisions.
weeks.project: Final data-intensive AI system is delivered with documentation and defense.

project.summary: Teams build one data-intensive system that ingests data, processes it through batch and streaming pipelines, and stores it in a replicated, partitioned distributed store. They integrate a machine learning model for serving, add data-quality and drift monitoring, and reason explicitly about consistency, fault tolerance, and architectural trade-offs throughout.

tools: Apache Kafka: stream and buffer event data
Apache Spark: run batch and distributed processing
Apache Flink: process streams with event-time semantics
PostgreSQL: store relational and derived data
Apache Cassandra: provide distributed partitioned storage
Apache Airflow: orchestrate data pipelines
dbt: transform and model warehouse data
MLflow: track and serve machine learning models
Feast: manage a feature store
Docker: package pipeline and serving components
Evidently: monitor data and model drift

references.title: Streaming Systems
references.authors: Tyler Akidau, Slava Chernyak, Reuven Lax

Dynamo: Amazon's Highly Available Key-value Store
references.authors: Giuseppe DeCandia, Deniz Hastorun, Madan Jampani, et al.

references.title: Hidden Technical Debt in Machine Learning Systems
references.authors: D. Sculley, Gary Holt, Daniel Golovin, et al.
